entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
An elective in Environmental Science, Advanced Nature Study requires independent interest as it is designed to be personalized by the student and requires some flexibility in scheduling due to the amount of fieldwork required beyond the regular lesson time.

This topic is included in the following course(s): Science: Advanced Nature Study

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Students should have at least 3 years of nature study experience and be comfortable with their nature journal.

rows.label: Advanced Nature Study Lessons
rows.scheduleInfo:
Advanced Nature Study Lessons
5 times/week 45 min

rows.label: Science: Advanced Nature Study
days: Advanced Nature Study Lessons*Advanced Nature Study Afternoon Work

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Advanced Nature Study Lessons
Download, save, bookmark, or print a copy of How to Teach Nature Journaling for regular use.

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Sections 1-3
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts, Journal

➜ INTRO
As we start our nature journaling adventure, it is important to remember local, state, and federal laws about collecting and specific concerns when in the field. Look up any local or state laws that you need to be aware of.

➜ READ
∞ Article Link: Leave No Trace (7 Principles)
∞ Article Link: Can I Keep This?

➜ INTRO
As we begin working through our Master of Field Arts book, we will start with a look at various tools that Roseann recommends. You may already have some of these tools or a variation of them. You may already use some or all of these tools in your nature study. The goal is to gather new ideas for tools and practices. There is only one tool that you were required to purchase for this course, and that was the Perspex Palette (see the supply list), as we will be using it quite a bit. For the rest of the items, you are free to evaluate and decide what you want to add to aid you in further observation and study. You can look up items mentioned in the chapter, and there will be links to additional or similar items, at times included in the lesson. 

➜ READ
p.7-17

➜ LOCATE
Check to see if you already own any of the items mentioned or something similar. If not, consider whether you want to purchase any items for further exploration. You may want to try out your phone's built-in magnifier. Search in your Apps to find the magnifier and look up how to use it. 
  
➜ VIEW
Take a look at some other interesting historical field notebooks.
∞ Article Link: Field Notebooks - Tumamoc Sketchbook

➜ JOURNAL
If you have any time left, use some form of magnification like a loupe, magnifying glass, binoculars, microscope, or your phone to view something from nature and replicate it in your journal.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
If interested, take a look at a loupe and these binoculars.
∞ Item Link: 10x and/or 30x (good for plant parts) Loupe 
(This item is from Amazon, but also take a look at Roseann's Discovery Kit on her website, which includes a basic loupe. Don't forget about her generous discount code offering 25% off any products in her store, which you can use throughout this course.)
∞ Item Link: Close Focus Binoculars recommended by John Muir Laws

links.text: Close Focus Binoculars recommended by John Muir Laws

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Sections 1-3
⍞ Materials: How to Teach Nature Journaling, Journal

➜ INTRO
Today is a journaling day. A prompt from John Muir Laws' book, How to Teach Nature Journaling, will provide some focus for today's journaling. You will be introduced to one or two prompts each week to give you many observation tools to use in your nature study. Use the link in the quick links to download a free digital copy of the book or purchase a paperback copy if you prefer. 

As you read the instructions each time, note the helps in teaching these skills to others. Be thinking about someone you could introduce nature journaling to, and/or share the joys of nature journaling with, and look for opportunities to show them what you are learning. It might be a family member, a neighbor, or a friend.
  
➜ READ OR VIEW
p.36-38 (in How to Teach Nature Journaling)
∞ Video Link: I Notice, I Wonder...

➜ JOURNAL
Head outside or bring something inside to practice INIWIRMO on (I notice, I wonder, It reminds me of).
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
If interested, take a look at Roseann's homemade leather nature journal cover. Maybe you want to make one for yourself!
∞ Article Link: Roseann's nature journal cover

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Sections 1-3
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts

➜ READ
p.18-26 skip p.25 as we will come back to this later. We will spend some dedicated time later in the course on collecting and drawing tracks in your journal

➜ LOCATE
There are many measuring and collecting options mentioned in the reading. See what you can find at home to use. Then, think through what you might want to purchase to start using in the field.
A pocket knife may be in a kitchen drawer, or a family member may have one that you could use. 

Also, consider what options are on your phone for measuring heights and widths as well as angles. Find the Measure App and look up how to use it. Practice using it for heights as well as angles. 

The End Notes start on p.159 and have many links for the items mentioned in the reading. You will need to figure out how you want to carry these items while you are in the field. See what bag options you have available and start taking your tools with you!
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS

∞ Link: Kestrel weather stations
∞ Link: Morakniv Clipper
∞ Link: Nature Journaling Field Bag (Could be a great Christmas or birthday gift idea!)

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Sections 1-3
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts

➜ INTRO
Let's step back for a moment to think about some term and school-year-long projects for this course. One of the joys of regular journaling is being able to see and observe seasonal changes. This is the study of phenology. Charlotte Mason had students create a Book of Firsts or add this component to their nature journals. Whenever you notice something for the first time in a season, such as the first frost, snowflakes, hurricane, plant blooms, bird, or butterfly, record the date on a perpetual calendar or in your nature journal. As you do this year after year, you start to notice patterns and deviations from patterns that are fascinating! 
 
➜ READ
p.157-158

➜ PLAN
Take some time to think about what locations around you would make an interesting Phenology Study. Choose one or two places to visit each week for the whole school year. This might be in your yard, down the street, or somewhere not far from where you live. You want it to be accessible so that you will be sure to go there every week to record changes and capture what you are seeing. Record the changes observed via words, pictures, sketches, measurements, etc., in your journal. Follow the suggestions in your reading on p.158. 

Then, choose a couple of items to do a term or single-season study, such as a single tree, plant, bird, or even a seed you grow in a pot. Take the time of year into account when choosing. This is described in your reading as a small or medium-scale study. But you are not limited to the examples in the book. Find something close to where you live that you are interested in studying. Choose when in the year you will do your studies. When you are ready, write down your plan for what you are studying and when in your week you will do your specific observations and journaling for your special studies. You might want to use a separate journal for your year-long study and term studies. It will be so interesting to read back over your journal entries for this one area or item and see the changes!

➜ READ
Another aspect of advanced nature study that we want to be a part of now and perhaps into the future is some sort of citizen science project. Take some time to read about some potential studies that pertain to your area using the links provided. Find one that you want to participate in. Nature's Notebook might have a project that relates to what you chose for your phenological study. Sign up for the project that interests you and follow the directions to get set up to start. Your observations are important and valuable contributions to science!
∞ Article Link: 9 Citizen Science Projects
∞ Article Link: Citizen Science  
∞ Article Link: 20 Citizen Science Projects
∞ Article Link: Nature's Notebook
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
If you are interested in adding a Book of Firsts to your different journals, take a look at Nathaniel Wheelwright and Bernd Heinrich's beautiful notebook.
∞ Link: The Naturalist's Notebook

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 8
lessons.body:
Sections 1-3
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts

➜ INTRO
Roseann is a seasoned naturalist and describes her method of taking field notes, journaling, and organizing her notes for later scientific study and writing projects. Don't get bogged down in the details of Part One (p.27-33). Reading about her process is helpful to see how a naturalist documents all of their work. Part 2, starting on p.34, will have some more practical information about metadata that you may want to incorporate into your journal pages.

➜ READ
p.27-40 
There is no right way to keep a journal. The main idea is to work in your journal at least four times a week to grow in all the skills and wonder of seeing what God has made! Try out new ideas and see what works best for you.

lessons.body:
Section 1-3
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts

➜ READ
p.60-63

➜ PRACTICE
Starting on p.60 with the Ink Tone, Texture, and Vegetation Palettes, practice each technique demonstrated. Make the charts and add your own additional textures. Then work on drawing with the outline and broken line techniques. Make several positive and negative space sketches of similar items. It takes some time to get good at seeing the shapes! Then, put the elements together and work on three versions of the same scene as Roseann did on p.62.

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 61
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Links, paints, paint brushes, journal

➜ INTRO
We will spend the next few weeks exploring the wonderful world of color! If you are new to adding color to your nature journal, Roseann's article will introduce you to the basics. If you are not new, consider trying out Roseann's palette of colors.

➜ READ
∞ Article Link: Meet the Minimalist Paint Set

➜ PRACTICE
If you haven't made a chart of how your particular colors work when mixing them, make a chart like in the article and experiment with your colors. It takes a while to get good at knowing your pigments and how to make the colors you need to match what you are looking at in nature. As Roseann points out, some paints are harder to use than others due to what is in them. If you are struggling, consider using the ones Roseann recommends. 

Follow what Roseann has put together for working on secondary colors, too.
∞ PDF Link: Color worksheets

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 62
lessons.body:
Sections 4-6
⍞ Materials: Master of Field Arts

➜ INTRO
People have been making paint from the earth for thousands of years. Notice what colors of dirt and rock are around you. Can you reproduce the colors with your paints? With appropriate precautions, you might want to experiment with making your own paints!

➜ READ
p.81-top of p.86

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: Earth Pigments
∞ Video Link: Lost Kingdoms of the Amazon (51:37)

Start at the 51:37 minute mark in this fascinating documentary on the lost civilizations of the Amazon to see some amazing rock art! The rest of it is worth watching as well at another time.

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 76
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Phenology Wheel Template, Journal

➜ INTRO
Hopefully, your special studies and citizen science projects are going well. Even if it is fall or winter outside, there are interesting projects to be involved in and creative nature journaling opportunities available. One is a phenology wheel of monthly colors. 

Building on the nature spots idea, try capturing more of the main colors outside during the month in your journal or on the wheel template, which you can paste into your journal. Then, you can continue the process all year. It might be the color of the sky, snow, and the green of an evergreen. Look around over several days and see what colors you notice. 
∞ PDF Link: Phenology Wheel template

Look at other examples of interesting things to use a phenology wheel to record.
∞ Website Link: Phenology Wheels

Roseann will walk through the reading for today, as well as how to make a cloud chart.
p.97-101

➜ JOURNAL
Roseann used the Clouds by Altitude chart in the links as her guide for making a cloud chart for her journal. There is a video from another source that also has a version of a cloud chart. Choose a format you like and begin work on your cloud chart. You can use watercolor with wax paper or crayons to draw the clouds as Roseann did first. Or, you can use toned paper and white gouache to paint in the cloud types. 
∞ Image Link: Clouds by Altitude
∞ Video Link: The 10 Main Clouds

Take some time to observe this week, depending on your weather, what rain, dew, fog, and rainbows actually look like. It is easy to have preconceived ideas of what these things look like, which will make it hard for us to draw what we see.

If you are looking for a Citizen Science Project related to tracking the weather, check out CoCoRaHS, which stands for Community Collaborative Rain, Hail, Snow Network.

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 99
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Journal

➜ INTRO
When the weather is not conducive to being outside, you can still nature journal virtually. Roseann has many interesting virtual field trips available on her website. Today we are going to go to Mars! It will take two days to work through the field trip. Stop the video and make your journal entries as Roseann is making hers.

➜ VIEW & JOURNAL
∞ Website Link: Let's Explore Mars!
lessons.teacherNotes:
• EXTRA HELPINGS
Something that you may be able to draw looking out your window is the moon! Roseann has a wonderful moon phase chart from a local hand-drawn book that you could use as a basis for drawing your own. Observe each night and draw what you see. Also, there is some great moon information at the NASA site.
∞ Image Link: Moon Phase Chart (scroll down)
∞ Website Link: Moon Phases

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 130
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Journal

➜ INTRO
Emily Dickinson wrote a poem called Bloom. She also put together a huge collection of pressed flowers she collected on her nature walks. Read the poem and view the herbarium that, surprisingly, is still preserved. Then watch the modern creative animation of both put together, and the backstory to its creation.

➜ READ
∞ Poem Link: Bloom
∞ Website Link: Harvard (Emily's Herbarium)
∞ Website Link: Musical Animation of Bloom

➜ JOURNAL
Find and add some favorite nature poems to your journal. Create some new ones!

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 145
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Journal

➜ INTRO
How is your year-long study going? I am sure you have collected a lot of great observations. If you still need to do a short-term project, consider what might be growing outside. Try to find something to observe from start to finish, like a caterpillar egg to a butterfly or a seed to a plant making new seeds. Sometimes you can see many stages of a plant at the same time, as John Muir Laws demonstrates in today's video. 

➜ VIEW
∞ Video Link: NJC Plant Timeline
 
➜ JOURNAL
See what you can find that would work well for a timeline.

lessons.title: 45m Advanced Nature Study Lessons - Lesson 146
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Journal

➜ INTRO
Think about what is right around you that you would like to know more about, and how to draw it in your journal. Look through John Muir Laws' list of topics and find something relevant to you that you are interested in.

➜ VIEW & PRACTICE
∞ Video Link: John Muir Laws Blog Archives

➜ JOURNAL
Enjoy!

lessons.body:

∞ Video Link: John Muir Laws Blog Archives

This week, we will explore the International Nature Journaling Week's 2023 program on the senses. Pick which of the senses you want to explore and click on that day's adventures. Watch the short introductory video at the top and then scroll down to the Nature Journaling Prompts and Ideas. Enjoy!

➜ VIEW

➜ JOURNAL

terms.content:
· Take pictures of 10 of your favorite journaling pages from the term.

· Describe some of the new skills or tools you have been experimenting with this term, and what you think about them. What have you liked and not liked, and where do you want to grow?

· What was one of your most interesting findings or observations from the term? What are you wondering about?

· Take pictures of 10 of your favorite journaling pages from the term.

· Describe some of the new skills or tools you have been experimenting with this term and what you think about them. What have you liked and not liked, and where do you want to grow?

· What was one of your most interesting findings or observations from the term? What are you wondering about?

· Take pictures of 10 of your favorite journaling pages from the term.

· Describe some of the new skills or tools you have been experimenting with this term and what you think about them. What have you liked and not liked, and where do you want to grow?

· Describe your year-long study. What plants, animals, etc., did you discover? Describe some of the changes you saw. What were some of the highlights? What surprised you? What questions did it generate? Include a few pictures of your journal pages about your study. Any ideas for other longer-term studies you could do?
